planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped shrubs in late winter or early spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; Korean spice viburnum blooms on old wood, so avoid pruning until after flowers fade in late spring.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; Korean spice viburnum is arguably the most sweetly fragrant of all flowering shrubs — the dense, rounded heads of small pink buds opening to white flowers in early spring produce an extraordinary spicy-sweet perfume that fills the garden on warm spring days and can be detected from 50 feet away. The fragrance is strongest on still mornings and in the evening warmth. The rounded shrub is neat and symmetrical, reaching 4–5 feet tall and wide over many years, with dull dark-green leaves that turn wine-red to burgundy in fall where summers are not too hot. It performs best in zones 4–7 where winter cold is reliable — in zone 8 and warmer, it often fails to bloom well due to insufficient winter chilling hours and may decline over time. Site it where the fragrance can be fully appreciated: near the entrance, beside a path, or outside a frequently used window. Prune lightly if needed immediately after flowering to maintain shape.

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped shrubs in late winter or early spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade; doublefile viburnum blooms on previous year's wood and should be pruned only immediately after flowering if shaping is needed.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; doublefile viburnum is one of the most architecturally striking flowering shrubs in cultivation — its strongly horizontal, tiered branch structure creates a layered, pagoda-like silhouette that is ornamental year-round even without flowers. In spring, rows of flat-topped lacecap flower clusters are borne in pairs ('doublefile') along the horizontal branches, covering the entire upper surface of each tier in white blossoms — a spectacular display. Red berries follow in summer, turning black and persisting into winter; they are attractive to birds, which rapidly consume them. In fall the leaves often turn wine-red to purplish-red. Popular cultivars include 'Shasta' (large, flat flower clusters and low, wide habit), 'Mariesii' (the most widely grown, strongly horizontal), and 'Summer Snowflake' (an extended blooming selection). Performs best in zone 5–7; in zone 8 it tolerates summer heat with afternoon shade and consistent moisture but may be short-lived.

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped shrubs in late winter or early spring in moist, very well-drained, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade; enkianthus requires the same acidic, organic, well-drained conditions as rhododendrons and azaleas and makes an outstanding companion in woodland or shade gardens with ericaceous plants.

directions: Plant in moist, very well-drained, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade; redvein enkianthus is an outstanding but underused deciduous shrub with four seasons of ornamental interest. In spring before the leaves emerge, pendulous clusters of small cream-to-pale-yellow bell-shaped flowers delicately striped with red veins are produced at branch tips — the striped pattern gives the species and cultivar names. The neat, whorled foliage arrangement on tiered branches creates an attractive, layered architecture through summer. In fall the foliage turns brilliant shades of orange, red, and yellow — among the best fall color of any flowering shrub. The upright, vase-shaped habit and slow growth make it suitable for smaller gardens. It thrives alongside azaleas, rhododendrons, mountain laurels, and other acid-loving shrubs in woodland settings, requiring the same culture: moist, acidic, organic, well-drained soil and partial shade. Performs best in zones 4–7 where reliable winter cold is available; marginally hardy in zone 8 in cool, shaded, moist sites.

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped shrubs in spring or early fall in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; Japanese holly is one of the most widely used foundation and hedge plants in the eastern US, valued for its fine texture, boxwood-like appearance, and tolerance of shearing.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; Japanese holly is a versatile evergreen shrub with small, dark-green, crenate (scalloped-edge) leaves that superficially resembles boxwood but is unrelated — it tolerates the same shearing and formal shaping, making it a popular boxwood substitute in regions where boxwood blight is problematic. The fine-textured, dense foliage is outstanding for formal hedges, mass plantings, and foundation shrubs. Small black berries are produced in fall and persist through winter but are inconspicuous. Key cultivars include 'Helleri' (very dwarf, spreading mound to 3×5 feet), 'Green Island' (open, spreading habit to 3–4 feet), 'Compacta' (dense, globular, 4–6 feet), and 'Sky Pencil' (dramatically narrow, columnar, 6–10 feet tall but only 2 feet wide). Japanese holly tolerates shade better than boxwood and performs reliably in zone 5–9. In zone 9, mulch root zone in summer to prevent heat stress.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Ghost fern establishes quickly and produces its distinctive silvery fronds from the first season, becoming more substantial each subsequent year.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Ghost fern is a hybrid cross between the Japanese painted fern (Athyrium niponicum var. pictum) and the lady fern (Athyrium filix-femina), combining the silver-frosted, upright-arching habit of the lady fern with the metallic silver-to-pewter coloring of the painted fern. The fronds are broader and more erect than Japanese painted fern but retain the glowing, silvery-white overlay that gives the plant its ghostly name. It was the Perennial Plant Association Plant of the Year in 2012. Ghost fern grows upright and arching rather than lying flat, creating a more vertical accent in the shade garden than its Japanese painted fern parent. It is generally considered hardier and more vigorous than Japanese painted fern and tolerates a wider range of soil conditions. Pair it with dark-leafed hostas, pulmonaria, epimedium, or brunnera for outstanding silver-and-contrast combinations in deep shade gardens.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained to average soil in partial to full shade; Sparkler sedge establishes quickly and maintains its striking variegated appearance throughout the growing season.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained to average soil in partial to full shade; Sparkler sedge is one of the most visually distinctive ornamental sedges available, forming a compact, upright mound of narrow, strap-like dark-green leaves boldly edged and striped with creamy-white margins that appear almost neon in shaded positions. The variegated foliage is at its most brilliant in partial shade; deep shade causes some fading but the plant remains ornamental. Sparkler is more heat-tolerant than most variegated sedges, performing well in zones 7–10 where many Carex cultivars struggle. The upright form and strongly contrasting variegation make it an outstanding accent or groundcover in Southern shade gardens, particularly under live oaks and in the dappled shade of urban tree canopies. In zone 9–10 it may be semi-evergreen or evergreen in mild winters; cut back any winter-damaged foliage in early spring before new growth emerges. Excellent container plant for shaded patios.
